required.title: [For Student — Read or Read Aloud] The Long Winter
required.author: Laura Ingalls Wilder

supplementary.title: [For Student] Biographies of athletes, musicians, or craftspeople

competence_markers.description: The student keeps commitments reliably — follow-through rate is improving and above 80% on tracked commitments
The student can name their emotional state in the moment and describe the difference between feeling and acting
The student demonstrates delayed gratification in observable choices — choosing better-later over easier-now regularly
The student works through boredom and discomfort to complete tasks without habitual quitting or avoidance
The weekly commitment review is honest and productive — the student identifies gaps without excuses and proposes corrections
The student manages emotional states without acting out — frustration does not produce yelling, shutting down, or lashing out as a pattern
The student can articulate why keeping commitments matters beyond personal consequence — understanding the relational and trust dimensions
The hard choice journal shows a pattern of deliberate self-governance over weeks and months

narration_prompts.prompt: What does it mean to keep a commitment?
narration_prompts.detail: Not the definition — what does it feel like? What happens when you keep one? What happens when you break one?

narration_prompts.prompt: Describe a time you felt a strong emotion and chose not to act on it.
narration_prompts.detail: What was the emotion? What did you want to do? What did you do instead? How did it turn out?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you chose delayed gratification.
narration_prompts.detail: What was the immediate option? What was the long-term option? Which did you choose and why? Was it worth it?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between suppressing an emotion and regulating it?
narration_prompts.detail: Give an example of each from your own experience.

narration_prompts.prompt: Look at your commitment tracking from the past month.
narration_prompts.detail: What is your follow-through rate? Where are the gaps? What patterns do you notice?

narration_prompts.prompt: Describe a time you worked through boredom to finish something.
narration_prompts.detail: What was the task? When did the boredom hit? How did you get through it?

cross_connections.description: Keeping commitments IS integrity in its most observable form. IC1.1-B2 builds the habit; IC1.3-B examines the principle. The student who tracks commitments here is producing the data that IC1.3-B's accountability work requires.
What happens when you break a commitment? IC1.4-B provides the framework for processing failure without collapse. IC1.1-B2 provides the daily context where that framework is needed.

Emotional regulation under stress is the core of IC3. IC1.1-B2 develops the same skill in daily life — the lower-stakes environment where the neural circuitry is built before the high-stakes environment tests it. IC3.1-B deepens the emotional vocabulary; B2 provides the daily practice ground.

Working through boredom and discomfort is the foundation of sustained work. IC2.2-B builds the internal quality standards; IC1.1-B2 builds the emotional capacity to maintain those standards when the work stops being interesting.

The emotional regulation developed here is directly tested in field exercises — building shelter in poor weather, maintaining fire through difficulty, sustaining effort when tired and uncomfortable. Fieldcraft is applied emotional regulation.

Martial arts training at Weathering Storms requires sustained effort through frustration, boredom during drills, and delayed gratification across months of practice before technique becomes reliable. The emotional regulation from B2 is the interior infrastructure that makes serious training possible.

content.why_this_matters:
You can plan your day. You can estimate how long things take. You can break a large task into steps and begin work without being told. These are the operational tools of self-governance.

This packet addresses the harder part: what happens inside you when self-governance is difficult.

You will make commitments you do not want to keep. You will feel angry, frustrated, bored, or discouraged — and need to act well anyway. You will face choices where the easy option is immediately rewarding and the right option pays off later, maybe much later, maybe in ways you cannot see.

These are not occasional challenges. They are daily reality. The person who can manage their internal states — who can feel frustration without acting from frustration, who can be bored without abandoning their work, who can choose the long path because they understand where it leads — has a capability that most adults lack. It is not emotional suppression. It is emotional regulation: feeling fully while choosing how to act.

This is the interior dimension of self-governance that Stretching Branches only began to develop through waiting and stopping. Weathering Storms makes it conscious, reflective, and practiced.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Keeping commitments** — treating a stated intention as a real obligation, especially when it becomes inconvenient
2. **Emotional regulation** — feeling without being controlled by feeling; choosing actions independently from emotional states
3. **Delayed gratification** — choosing the harder path now for the better outcome later
4. **Working through boredom and discomfort** — continuing to do what must be done when it is not interesting or pleasant
5. **Honest self-assessment of follow-through** — tracking what you said versus what you did, without excuses
6. **Managing emotional states without acting out** — distinguishing between what you feel and what you do about it
content.core_content:
### Part 1: What a Commitment Actually Is

When you say "I will do this," something real happens. Not legally. Not magically. But relationally and personally. You have created a debt — a gap between the present (where the thing is not done) and the future you promised (where the thing is done). Until you close that gap, the commitment exists.

Most people treat commitments casually. "I'll try." "I'll do my best." "I should be able to." These are not commitments. They are intentions dressed as commitments. A real commitment sounds like: "I will do this by Thursday." Full stop. Not "unless something comes up." Not "if I feel like it." The commitment stands until it is fulfilled or explicitly renegotiated.

**The rules of commitment:**
1. Do not commit to what you cannot deliver. It is better to say "I don't think I can do that" than to say "sure" and fail.
2. Once committed, do what you said. The inconvenience is not a reason to break the commitment — it is the test of whether the commitment was real.
3. If you realize you cannot meet a commitment, say so immediately — not when the deadline arrives. Renegotiation with advance notice is honest. Failure without warning is a breach of trust.
4. Track your commitments. Write them down. Know what you owe.

### Part 2: Emotional Regulation — Not Suppression

There is a critical difference between suppressing emotions and regulating them.

**Suppression** says: "Don't feel that." It pushes the emotion down, pretends it is not there. Suppression does not work — the emotion comes out sideways as irritability, withdrawal, or explosion at something unrelated.

**Regulation** says: "I feel that. Now: what am I going to do?" It acknowledges the emotion fully and then chooses an action that is not dictated by the emotion.

You will feel angry and choose not to yell. You will feel bored and choose to continue working. You will feel frustrated and choose to try again instead of quitting. You will feel like giving up and choose not to.

None of this requires you to stop feeling. The feeling is real and it is yours. The question is: does the feeling choose your behavior, or do you?

**The gap:** Between a feeling and an action, there is a space. It might be very small — a fraction of a second. In that space, you make a choice. Regulation is the practice of widening that space until the choice is conscious rather than automatic.

**How to practice:**
- When you notice a strong emotion (anger, frustration, boredom, anxiety), name it silently: "I am angry right now." The naming itself creates distance.
- Ask yourself: "What do I want to do?" (This is the impulse.) Then ask: "What should I do?" (This is the regulated response.) They are often different.
- If the emotion is very strong, create physical space: step outside, take five slow breaths, get a drink of water. You are not running from the emotion. You are giving yourself the gap you need to choose.

### Part 3: Delayed Gratification — Choosing the Long Game

Delayed gratification means choosing a larger, later reward over a smaller, immediate one. It is one of the most studied and most predictive human capacities — research consistently shows that the ability to delay gratification in childhood predicts academic success, health, financial stability, and relationship quality decades later.

**Examples:**
- Studying for a test you could skip (the grade comes later; the relief of not studying comes now)
- Practicing a skill when you could be playing (mastery comes later; fun comes now)
- Saving money instead of spending it (the purchase you can afford later; the pleasure of spending now)
- Choosing the difficult but correct option over the easy but inadequate one

**Why it is hard:** Your brain is wired to prefer immediate rewards. This is not a character flaw — it is a feature of human neurology that evolved for survival in environments where the future was uncertain. But you do not live in that environment. You live in a world where the future is reasonably predictable, and the person who invests in it has enormous advantages over the person who does not.

**How to practice:**
- When faced with a choice between easy-now and better-later, identify both options explicitly: "I can play now, or I can finish my work and play later with it off my mind."
- Notice what happens after you choose the delayed option: the relief of completion, the satisfaction of self-governance, the absence of the guilt that follows avoidance.
- Stack small wins. You do not develop delayed gratification by making one enormous sacrifice. You develop it by making hundreds of small choices — finishing before playing, working before resting, preparing before enjoying.

### Part 4: Working Through Boredom and Discomfort

Not everything you need to do will be interesting. Most important work contains stretches that are tedious, repetitive, or uncomfortable. The person who can only work when motivated will accomplish very little — because motivation is intermittent, unreliable, and usually absent precisely when the work is most important.

**The reality:** Motivation starts things. Discipline finishes them. You cannot wait to feel like doing the work. You do the work, and sometimes the feeling follows. Sometimes it does not. The work gets done either way.

**Boredom is not a crisis.** It is a signal that the task does not offer the immediate stimulation your brain prefers. That is information, not an emergency. The task still needs to be done. Do it.

**Discomfort is not a crisis.** Physical discomfort (you are tired, the weather is bad, the chair is uncomfortable) and emotional discomfort (you are frustrated, the material is hard, you do not understand) are real but they are not reasons to stop. They are conditions to work through.

**The practice:** When you feel bored or uncomfortable during a task, notice the feeling, name it ("I am bored"), and continue working. That is the entire technique. It sounds trivially simple. It is extraordinarily hard to do consistently. It is also the single most differentiating habit between people who build things and people who do not.

### Part 5: Honest Self-Assessment

Self-governance requires honesty about your own performance. Not harsh self-criticism. Not generous self-congratulation. Accurate assessment.

**The weekly commitment review:**
At the end of each week, look at what you committed to and what you delivered.

| What I said I'd do | Did I do it? | If not, why? |
|---|---|---|
| (specific commitment) | Yes / No / Partially | (honest reason) |

**The honest reason matters.** "I didn't have time" is almost never true — you had time, but you chose to spend it differently. "I forgot" means you did not write it down or check your plan. "It was harder than I expected" is legitimate and useful — it calibrates your future commitments.

No excuses. No self-flagellation. Just accurate information about the gap between intention and execution. Over time, the gap narrows — not because the work gets easier, but because your self-knowledge gets more accurate and your follow-through gets more reliable.
content.formative_practices:
### Practice 1: Commitment Tracking [Ongoing]
Keep a running list of commitments — to your mentor, to your family, to yourself. Check them off as completed. Review weekly. The list is your accountability record.

### Practice 2: Emotion Naming [Daily]
At least once a day, when you feel a strong emotion, name it silently before acting. "I am frustrated." "I am bored." "I am angry." The naming is the practice. Over time, the gap between feeling and action widens.

### Practice 3: The Delayed Gratification Log [Weekly]
Each week, identify at least one choice you made to delay gratification. Write it down: what was the easy option, what was the better-later option, which did you choose, and what happened? Track the pattern over weeks.

### Practice 4: Boredom Work-Through [As Needed]
When you feel bored during a task, note the time. Continue working. Note when the boredom lifts (it usually does within 10-15 minutes). If it does not lift, you still completed the task. Record the experience.

### Practice 5: Weekly Commitment Review [Weekly, With Mentor]
Structured review of commitments made vs. commitments kept. No lecturing from the mentor. The student identifies the gaps and proposes what to change. The mentor asks clarifying questions: "What got in the way? What would you do differently? Was the commitment realistic?"

### Practice 6: The Hard Choice Journal [Ongoing]
A dedicated section of your journal (or a separate notebook) where you record moments you chose the harder, better option over the easier, worse one. These are small victories that compound. Looking back at them over months builds identity: "I am the kind of person who does the hard thing."

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Commitment Follow-Through
**Developing well:** The student's tracked commitment completion rate is above 80% and improving. When they fail, they can identify why without excuses. They are beginning to calibrate — making fewer commitments they cannot keep rather than more commitments they break.

**Developing slowly:** Follow-through is below 60% with no upward trend. The student makes commitments easily but treats them as aspirational rather than obligatory. Excuses are the default explanation for missed commitments.

### 2. Emotional Regulation
**Developing well:** The student can name their emotional state in the moment — "I'm frustrated" or "I'm bored" — at least some of the time. When they feel a strong emotion, there is an observable gap between the feeling and their action. They may still act from emotion sometimes, but the trend is toward choosing their response.

**Developing slowly:** Emotions produce immediate behavioral responses without any visible pause. The student cannot name what they are feeling when asked. Frustration always produces quitting or outbursts. Boredom always produces task abandonment.

### 3. Delayed Gratification
**Developing well:** The student makes observable choices to do the harder thing before the easier thing — finishing work before play, completing a commitment before starting something fun. They can articulate why they made that choice. The delayed gratification log shows a pattern over weeks.

**Developing slowly:** The student consistently chooses the immediate reward. They cannot identify examples of delayed gratification from their own life. When asked why they chose the easy option, the answer is some form of "I wanted to."

### 4. Working Through Boredom
**Developing well:** The student encounters boring or tedious work and continues. They may express boredom — "This is boring" — but do not use boredom as a reason to stop. They are discovering that boredom usually passes within 10-15 minutes.

**Developing slowly:** Boredom is treated as a legitimate reason to abandon a task. The student cannot sustain effort on uninteresting work for more than a few minutes. "I'm bored" functions as a request to do something else.

### 5. Honest Self-Assessment
**Developing well:** The weekly commitment review produces accurate self-knowledge. The student identifies gaps between intention and action without excuses and without excessive self-criticism. They propose realistic corrections. The review conversation is productive and does not require the mentor to extract information.

**Developing slowly:** The review is either defensive ("It wasn't my fault") or performative ("Everything was fine"). The student cannot identify patterns in their own behavior. Proposed corrections are vague ("I'll try harder") rather than specific ("I'll do the reading first thing instead of after lunch").

### 6. Managing Emotions Without Acting Out
**Developing well:** Frustration, anger, and disappointment are expressed verbally or processed internally rather than acted out. The student does not yell, slam things, shut down, or lash out as a pattern. They may still have occasional failures — the trend, not perfection, is the measure.

**Developing slowly:** Strong emotions consistently produce behavioral responses: door slamming, yelling, prolonged sulking, refusal to engage. The student does not distinguish between feeling angry and acting angry.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Treating Regulation as Suppression
If your response to the student's frustration is "calm down" or "stop being upset," you are teaching suppression, not regulation. The student packet explicitly distinguishes these. Regulation acknowledges the feeling and chooses the action. Suppression denies the feeling. A student who hears "stop being upset" repeatedly learns to hide their emotions, not to manage them. The emotions come out elsewhere — irritability, withdrawal, or explosion over something unrelated.

**Instead:** "I can see you're frustrated. That's fine. What are you going to do?" The feeling is validated. The choice is returned to them.

### Rescuing from Natural Consequences
The student committed to finishing a project by Saturday. It is Friday night and they have barely started. The temptation: extend the deadline, reduce the scope, stay up late helping them, or declare that "we'll just do it next week."

Every rescue teaches the same lesson: commitments are not real. Someone will adjust reality when I fail to meet my obligations. The correct response is to let Saturday arrive with the project incomplete. The discomfort of that incompleteness — especially if there are real consequences (they cannot start the next thing, they must explain the gap in their review) — is the teacher.

**Exception:** If the commitment failure was caused by something genuinely outside the student's control (illness, family emergency, a dependency that fell through), renegotiation is appropriate. The student should still identify what happened and how to handle similar situations in the future. But this is rare. Most commitment failures are choices — not circumstances.

### Lecturing During the Weekly Review
The review is the student's self-assessment, not your correction session. If you spend the review telling the student what they did wrong, you have converted a self-governance practice into a performance review administered by an authority figure. The student will learn to manage your perception rather than their behavior.

**The discipline:** Ask questions. Listen to answers. Ask follow-up questions. Offer your observation only if the student cannot see a pattern that is obvious and consequential: "I notice that the commitments you break are almost always the ones scheduled for afternoon. Do you see that?" Even then, frame it as a question they answer, not a conclusion you deliver.

### Setting Expectations Too High for Emotional Regulation
Emotional regulation develops over years. A 9-year-old who can name their emotion some of the time and pause before acting on it occasionally is developing well. A 9-year-old who has perfect emotional composure at all times is either unusually mature or suppressing. Do not mistake quiet compliance for regulation.

The markers in this packet are trends, not standards. You are looking for the gap between feeling and action to widen over months — not for the gap to be fully established by the end of a semester.

### Ignoring the Distinction Between Can't and Won't
Some students genuinely struggle with emotional regulation or delayed gratification due to neurological differences. The student who is trying hard, engaging honestly with the practices, and making zero progress after months of effort may need professional support — not more practice. ADHD, anxiety, sensory processing differences, and other conditions can make the gap between feeling and action physiologically harder to widen.

This does not mean abandoning the goal. It means modifying the approach: shorter commitment horizons, explicit external cues for transitions, reduced task loads during emotionally demanding periods, and possibly professional evaluation. The B1 Mentor Guide addresses this for executive function; the same principle applies here for emotional regulation.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: What Does It Mean to Keep a Commitment?
**Strong:** Moves beyond definition to experience. Describes what it feels like to keep one (relief, pride, trust built) and what it feels like to break one (guilt, loss of trust, the weight of the unfulfilled obligation). May give a specific example.
**Weak:** Recites the definition from the packet. "It means you do what you said." No personal experience, no felt understanding.

### Prompt 2: Feeling Without Acting
**Strong:** Names a specific emotion and a specific moment. Describes the impulse ("I wanted to yell" or "I wanted to quit") and the chosen action ("I took a breath and kept going" or "I told my sister I was angry instead of hitting her"). Shows awareness of the gap between feeling and action.
**Weak:** Cannot recall an example, or describes a situation where they did act on the emotion and does not recognize it as such.

### Prompt 3: Delayed Gratification
**Strong:** Gives a concrete example with both options clearly stated. Can articulate why they chose the delayed option and whether it was worth it in retrospect. Shows emerging understanding that the delayed choice usually feels better after the fact.
**Weak:** Cannot identify an example. Or gives an example that was actually an external requirement ("I had to do my homework before playing") rather than a genuine choice they made.

### Prompt 4: Suppression vs. Regulation
**Strong:** Can explain the difference clearly: suppression pushes the feeling down; regulation feels the feeling and chooses the action. Gives an example of each — ideally from their own life. May describe a time they tried suppression and it did not work (the feeling came back or came out differently).
**Weak:** Conflates the two. "Regulation is when you don't feel angry." This indicates the concept has not landed — they are still hearing "don't feel that" rather than "feel it and choose."

### Prompt 5: Commitment Tracking Data
**Strong:** Has actual data — numbers, percentages, patterns. "I kept 7 out of 9 commitments this week. The two I missed were both afternoon study sessions. I think I'm scheduling too much in the afternoon." This is the kind of honest, data-driven self-assessment that indicates the practice is working.
**Weak:** "It was mostly good." No data, no pattern recognition, no specifics.

### Prompt 6: Working Through Boredom
**Strong:** Describes a specific task, names the moment boredom arrived, describes the experience of continuing anyway. May note that the boredom lifted after a while, or that it did not lift but the task was still completed. Shows that boredom is understood as a condition to work through, not a crisis to resolve.
**Weak:** "I just do boring stuff sometimes." No specificity, no awareness of the internal experience.
